Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative equational clause with universal quantifier nochi and relative marker tein; adjective-like predicate kostik.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Not everything that looks valuable or impressive is truly good, useful, or trustworthy.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Amo nochi tlen nesi kuali melahuak kuali.
Usage Contexts: Advice, trade, family counsel, politics, humor about appearances.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Regional proverb-style expression shaped by market life in the Sierra Norte, where appearance, bargaining, and practical value are constantly judged.
First Example Sentence: Amo ximokajkayahua ika in kuajtomin tein kipiya; amo nochi tein petlani kostik.
Second Example Sentence: Okachi kuali tikitas seki tonali itech tekitl, pampa amo nochi tein petlani kostik.
Third Example Sentence: Nonantsin techilwia, amo nochi tein petlani kostik, ihkuak tikohuas se tlakualoni.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verbal clause with transitive verb kipiya, possessed noun yolo, and locative phrase itech ima.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Said of a generous, skillful, or caring person whose work shows heart and dedication.
Usage Contexts: Crafts, cooking, family praise, workplace evaluation, community service.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connects heart-centered moral value with handwork, especially embroidery, planting, cooking, and communal labor in Puebla towns.
First Example Sentence: Tokni kipiya yolo itech ima, yehika kuali kichihua nochi in tlakualis.
Second Example Sentence: In sihuat tekitini kipiya yolo itech ima, amo san niman kichihua.
Third Example Sentence: Tlen okichihkej kaltenko neski kuali, pampa okipixkej yolo itech inma.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Perfective transitive clause with animal subject metaphor kuauhtli and locative phrase ipan iyolik.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone acted with courage and inner strength when facing danger or shame.
Usage Contexts: Storytelling, public praise, conflict resolution, ceremonial speech.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on the eagle as a Mesoamerican symbol of force, vision, and noble courage, adapted in contemporary community narration.
First Example Sentence: Ihkuak nochi momawtiayaj, Pedro okicholo in kuauhtli ipan iyolik.
Second Example Sentence: Tehhuan tikitakej ken okicholo in kuauhtli ipan iyolik huan okipalehui ichampoyohuan.
Third Example Sentence: Amo okilnamik in pinahistli; okicholo in kuauhtli ipan iyolik.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Reflexive/intransitive predicate moketsa with instrumental-comitative preposition ika and numeral phrase ome imetz.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To stand firm, act independently, or take responsibility without relying on others.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mosentlalia huan kichihua itekiw ika iselti.
Usage Contexts: Workplace, family advice, youth guidance, leadership.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Based on the embodied image of standing on two feet, often used in advice about maturity, work, and household responsibility.
First Example Sentence: Ximoketsa ika ome mometz huan xiktlalia motekiw axkan.
Second Example Sentence: Yeh moketsa ika ome imetz, pampa kuali kimati tlen kineki.
Third Example Sentence: Notajtsin nechilwiaya ma nimoketsa ika ome nometz ihkuak nipehuas nitekitis.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Coordinated clause with transitive verbs kitoka and kichiaya; agricultural object etl paired with metaphorical object xochitl.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone expects beautiful results without respecting the effort, time, or nature of the work.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kineki kuali tlamantli, maski amo kuali kitekitilia.
Usage Contexts: Farming, education, parenting, criticism of unrealistic plans.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses milpa imagery familiar in Highland Puebla: each seed has its time, and careless planting cannot produce the desired harvest.
First Example Sentence: In telpokatl kitoka etl, kichiaya xochitl, pampa amo momachtia huan kineki kuali nota.
Second Example Sentence: Amo xikchihua kemi tikitoka etl huan tikchiaya xochitl; xitekitihua ika yolsewilistli.
Third Example Sentence: Yehhuan okinekiaj kuali tlasentilistli, pero okitokakej etl huan okichiayaj xochitl.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive clause with personified subject ehekatl and possessed object itlakual; metaphorical weather agent.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Plans, money, food, or time disappeared because of disorder, waste, or neglect.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Opoliwik tlen okatka ika amo kuali tlayejyekolistli.
Usage Contexts: Household economy, market talk, project planning, mild complaint.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Wind is a frequent metaphor for loss, dispersal, and unpredictability in mountain weather and agricultural life.
First Example Sentence: Amo tikpiyaj ok tomin, kemi okikua itlakual in ehekatl.
Second Example Sentence: Tein otikchiayaj para ilwitl opoliwik; okikua itlakual in ehekatl.
Third Example Sentence: Tlen okitlalijkej ipan amatl amo okiskej, pampa okikua itlakual in ehekatl.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Reflexive/intransitive clause with locative phrase itech tlahko ohtli.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To avoid choosing sides, delay a decision, or remain in an uncertain middle position.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Amo motlalia kanaj; amo kimati tlen kinekis.
Usage Contexts: Politics, family disputes, workplace decisions, negotiations.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Road imagery is common in mountainous communities, where choosing the right path has practical and moral weight.
First Example Sentence: Tekihuaj motlalia itech tlahko ohtli huan amo techilwia tlen kichihuas.
Second Example Sentence: Amo ximotlali itech tlahko ohtli; xinechilwi kampa tias.
Third Example Sentence: Yeh okachi moneki moyolchikahuas, pampa semi motlalia itech tlahko ohtli.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verbal clause with transitive kipiya, noun istatl, and locative-possessive complement itech itlahtol.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone speaks with sharpness, wit, sarcasm, or a sting that others feel immediately.
Usage Contexts: Humor, teasing, political criticism, family banter, storytelling.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Salt is linked to taste, preservation, and sting; the expression maps flavor onto speech in everyday oral humor.
First Example Sentence: Kualli xikkaki, pampa in wewentsin kipiya istatl itech itlahtol.
Second Example Sentence: Yeh amo kualani, pero kipiya istatl itech itlahtol ihkuak techtlatlania.
Third Example Sentence: Ipan tlanonotsalistli, María okipixki istatl itech itlahtol huan nochi owetzkakej.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Comparative clause with reflexive/intransitive mokahua, comparative particle kemi, and locative phrase ipan kiowitl.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone remains stubbornly in place, unmoved by advice, pressure, or changing conditions.
Usage Contexts: Family teasing, workplace frustration, community meetings, humor.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The metate is a durable household object; placing it in rain intensifies the image of heavy, unmoving stubbornness.
First Example Sentence: Nokni mokahua kemi metlatl ipan kiowitl, amo kikaki ken tikilwiaj.
Second Example Sentence: Maski tlayekana, in tlakatl mokahua kemi metlatl ipan kiowitl.
Third Example Sentence: Amo xieto kemi metlatl ipan kiowitl; xikkaki tein mitsilwiaj.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Transitive clause with verb kichihua and paradoxical instrumental phrase ika atl.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To try to solve a problem in a way that contradicts itself or makes the task impossible.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Kichihua se tlamantli ika se tlamantli tein amo kipalehuia.
Usage Contexts: Technical discussion, project management, education, irony, criticism.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on elemental contrast between fire and water, a transparent metaphor for incompatible methods or self-defeating plans.
First Example Sentence: Tiknekij tlen amo huelis, kemi tikchihuas tletl ika atl.
Second Example Sentence: In tlayejyekolistli amo kuali, pampa kichihua tletl ika atl.
Third Example Sentence: Amo ma tikchihuakah tletl ika atl; achto ma tikmotlatlanilikah tlen moneki.
